extra.reasoning:
The property globally(opted_out implies not sent) holds. It is an inductive invariant preserved by every transition.

Initial state: opted_out=false, so the implication is vacuously true.

The only transition that can set sent:=true is t2 (send_attempt), and its guard requires not opted_out; t2 leaves opted_out unchanged. So immediately after t2, opted_out=false and the implication holds.

The only transition that sets opted_out:=true is t1 (optout), which simultaneously sets sent:=false. So immediately after t1, sent=false and the implication holds.

t3 (send_attempt while opted_out) sets sent:=false, and t4 (tick) sets sent:=false; neither can create the bad combination.

opted_out is monotonic: no transition ever sets it back to false. Therefore, once opted_out=true, the implication can only be broken by setting sent:=true, which requires t2 — but t2 is guarded by not opted_out and is disabled. t3/t4 only clear sent. Hence no reachable state (within or beyond 14 events) can have opted_out=true and sent=true. The bad state is unreachable; the safety property holds for the entire input space.
